planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain consistently above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and bring indoors before temperatures approach 50°F. Micans is a velvety-leaved cultivar of Heartleaf Philodendron — the same species as the standard green Heartleaf Philodendron but with an iridescent, bronze-green, velvety texture on the leaf surface that catches and reflects light in an unusual way. Stem cuttings root easily in water or moist propagating mix, making propagation simple. In zone 9 the warm, humid summer promotes lush, rapid growth on shaded patios, pergolas, and covered outdoor living spaces.

directions: Plant in bright indirect light to shade — never in direct sun, which bleaches and scorches the velvety leaf surface — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil; Micans is a velvety-leaved cultivar of Heartleaf Philodendron (Philodendron hederaceum) producing heart-shaped leaves in bronze-green with a distinctive iridescent, velvet-textured surface that catches light differently at different angles — shifting from deep bronze-green in direct viewing to a warmer, almost copper-gold sheen when light strikes the leaf surface obliquely across the fine surface trichomes that create the velvety texture. The leaf color and texture of Micans distinguishes it clearly from the standard smooth, glossy-green Heartleaf Philodendron, creating a more sophisticated, jewel-like foliage quality that has made it one of the most sought-after trailing philodendrons among contemporary plant collectors. New growth emerges in a warmer pinkish-bronze before deepening to the mature bronze-green, creating an additional decorative effect as the plant actively grows. The trailing, vining habit — identical to the parent species — makes Philodendron Micans excellent for hanging baskets, trailing container edges, and shaded climbing situations where the long, velvety stems cascade attractively over the container lip.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron hederaceum Cultivars for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Vines for Gulf South Shade
American Horticultural Society — Philodendron Species and Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain consistently above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and bring indoors before temperatures approach 50°F. Neon Robusta produces its most vivid pink coloration in bright indirect light; deep shade causes the foliage to revert toward lighter green with less pink saturation. Stem cuttings root easily in water or moist propagating mix. As the plant matures it transitions from a compact, upright bushy form to a trailing or climbing vine — the juvenile arrowhead-shaped leaves giving way to larger, multi-lobed adult leaves if the plant is allowed to climb. In zone 9 the warm summer humidity promotes vigorous growth.

directions: Plant in bright indirect light to shade — avoid direct sun, which bleaches the distinctive pink coloration — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist potting mix; Neon Robusta is a pink-leaved cultivar of Arrowhead Plant (Syngonium podophyllum) producing distinctive arrowhead-shaped leaves in a warm rose-pink to dusty mauve-pink that is one of the most unusual and distinctive foliage colors available among commonly grown shade tropical plants. The pink coloration is evenly distributed across the entire leaf surface — not a variegation pattern of green-and-pink like many other pink-leaved plants, but a full-leaf pink that makes Neon Robusta a genuinely unusual and striking specimen in shade garden collections. Arrowhead plants (Syngonium podophyllum) are among the most adaptable and genuinely shade-tolerant tropical foliage plants available — surviving in conditions ranging from bright indirect light to deep shade while maintaining reasonable growth rate and foliage quality. The compact, bushy juvenile form of Neon Robusta (before the plant begins to vine) makes it particularly useful for small containers, terrariums, and tight shade planting situations where a larger trailing plant would be impractical.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Syngonium podophyllum for Florida Interiors and Shade
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pink-Foliaged Tropicals for Gulf South Shade
American Horticultural Society — Syngonium Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Britt-Marie Crawford requires consistently moist soil and is intolerant of dry conditions — it wilts dramatically in heat and drought and must have adequate moisture to recover. In zone 9 site in deep shade with consistent moisture — near a water feature, pond edge, or other reliably moist location — to maintain the large, dramatic foliage without wilting stress. The dark purple undersides of the large leaves are most visible when viewed from below or at a low angle — positioning plants where the undersides are visible enhances the two-toned effect. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become crowded.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil — Britt-Marie Crawford Ligularia demands cool, moist, shaded conditions and will wilt dramatically in heat, drought, or direct sun; Britt-Marie Crawford is an exceptionally dark-leaved cultivar of Big-Leaf Ligularia (Ligularia dentata), producing some of the largest and most dramatically dark foliage available in any shade-tolerant hardy perennial — the large, kidney-shaped leaves reaching 12–18 inches across in mature plants, with an upper surface in deep brownish-purple to near-black and a contrasting brilliant deep purple-red on the leaf undersides. The sheer scale of the leaves combined with the extreme dark coloration creates a dramatically bold, moody shade garden statement that few other plants can replicate — the large, dark leaves absorbing and deepening the light quality of the shaded environment rather than reflecting it. In late summer Britt-Marie Crawford produces tall stems bearing clusters of vivid orange-yellow daisy flowers that create a striking bicolor contrast with the near-black foliage — one of the most unexpected and dramatic color combinations in the shade perennial garden. For zone 8 and the warmest zone 9 microclimates with reliable moisture and deep shade, Ligularia provides an extraordinary shade garden presence unmatched by most other commonly grown shade perennials.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Ligularia dentata for Southeastern Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Dark-Leaved Shade Perennials
American Horticultural Society — Ligularia Species and Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Place outdoors in shade to partial shade after temperatures remain above 55°F; in zone 9 use outdoors April–October and bring indoors when temperatures approach 45°F. Laurentii is a variegated cultivar with the distinctive bright yellow margin stripe — this variegation is propagated by division only (leaf cuttings revert to plain green). Dracaena trifasciata 'Laurentii' (formerly Sansevieria trifasciata 'Laurentii') is one of the most studied plants for indoor air quality — NASA's Clean Air Study identified it as effective at removing formaldehyde, benzene, and other VOCs from interior air. The upright, strap-leaved form takes up minimal floor space relative to its visual impact, making it effective in confined container situations.

directions: Plant in shade to bright indirect light — tolerates extremely low light conditions where most plants cannot survive, while also adapting to bright indirect light — in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil or potting mix; Variegated Snake Plant (Dracaena trifasciata 'Laurentii') is the classic variegated form of Mother-in-Law's Tongue, producing the familiar upright, stiff, strap-shaped leaves with the distinctive pattern of dark green cross-banding on a lighter green ground — and in the 'Laurentii' form, an additional bright golden-yellow stripe running the full length of both leaf margins. The combination of the detailed cross-banded central pattern and the bold, clean yellow margin stripe creates a more visually striking and decorative effect than the plain green species, making 'Laurentii' the most commonly sold and planted snake plant variant worldwide. Dracaena trifasciata tolerates neglect — infrequent watering, low light, temperature fluctuations, and irregular fertilization — at levels that would kill most other decorative foliage plants, making it genuinely practical for situations (covered porches, low-light indoor corners, weekend homes) where consistent care cannot be guaranteed. The extremely low water requirement (tolerating complete drought for weeks) combined with the low-light tolerance and the clean, architectural upright form makes the Variegated Snake Plant one of the most practically useful shade and low-light ornamentals available.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dracaena trifasciata for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Foliage Plants for Gulf South
NASA Clean Air Study — Interior Foliage Plants for Air Quality

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–June. Egyptian Riverhemp is a fast-growing, warm-season legume used as a green manure, forage crop, and soil improvement cover crop in tropical and subtropical regions. Scarify seeds (nick seed coat or soak in hot water for 30 minutes) to improve germination rate from the naturally hard seed coat. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, inoculate seed with appropriate Rhizobium inoculant before planting for maximum N-fixation. Plants grow extremely fast — reaching 6–10 feet in 60–90 days in zone 9 — making rapid canopy cover and high biomass production for incorporation or cutting. Chop and incorporate into soil before seed set to prevent naturalization; it is potentially invasive in wetlands.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in moist to moderately well-drained, low-fertility soil — Sesbania sesban is adapted to waterlogged, flooded, and poor soils where most other cover crops fail; inoculate seed with appropriate Rhizobium before planting; Egyptian Riverhemp is one of the fastest-growing and most productive warm-season nitrogen-fixing cover crops available for tropical and subtropical climates — capable of producing 5–10 tons of dry biomass per acre per season and fixing 100–200 pounds of nitrogen per acre when properly inoculated, making it among the most efficient green manure crops for improving depleted soils in zone 9 and the tropical belt. The rapid growth rate — reaching 6–10 feet in 60–90 days — produces a dense, shading canopy that suppresses weeds effectively while simultaneously building organic matter and fixing atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis with Rhizobium bacteria. The combination of nitrogen fixation, rapid biomass production, weed suppression, and adaptation to waterlogged and flooded soils that defeat other cover crops makes Sesbania sesban one of the most valuable green manure crops for difficult, wet, low-fertility subtropical soils. Chop and incorporate the green material 60–90 days after sowing, before seed set — the succulent stems and leaves decompose rapidly, releasing nitrogen and organic matter into the soil within 2–4 weeks of incorporation.
sources: USDA National Resources Conservation Service — Cover Crop Species: Sesbania sesban
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Warm-Season Cover Crops for Florida
FAO — Sesbania sesban as Green Manure and Forage Crop in Tropical Agriculture
